steps: Prune and destroy infected leaves and twigs during dormant season.
Apply a recommended fungicide at green tip, pink, and petal-fall stages.
Remove fallen leaves from the orchard floor to reduce inoculum.
Avoid overhead irrigation that keeps foliage wet for long periods.
prevention: Plant resistant apple varieties where available.
Maintain good air circulation by proper pruning and spacing.
Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization that encourages dense foliage.
Monitor orchards regularly, especially in wet spring conditions.
Apple___Apple_scab.expertNote: Apple scab thrives in cool, wet weather. Early-season protection is critical to prevent multiple infection cycles.

steps: Remove and destroy mummified fruit and cankered branches.
Prune back affected limbs to healthy tissue and disinfect tools.
Apply appropriate fungicides during early fruit development stages.
Improve orchard sanitation by removing dead wood and prunings.
prevention: Avoid leaving pruned branches or mummies in or near the orchard.
Maintain tree vigor through balanced fertilization and watering.
Inspect trees regularly for early signs of cankers and fruit infections.
Choose planting sites with good air drainage and sun exposure.
Apple___Black_rot.expertNote: Black rot can overwinter in cankers and mummified fruit; sanitation is one of the most effective management strategies.

steps: Remove and destroy infected leaves, shoots, and mummified berries.
Apply fungicides at early shoot growth, pre-bloom, and post-bloom as needed.
Train vines to improve air movement and light penetration.
Avoid overhead irrigation that prolongs leaf wetness.
prevention: Use resistant or tolerant grape cultivars where possible.
Maintain clean vineyard floors, removing dropped fruit and debris.
Prune annually to keep canopy open and well-structured.
Monitor weather conditions and apply fungicides preventively in wet seasons.
Grape___Black_rot.expertNote: Black rot infections often start on young tissues. Protecting early-season growth greatly reduces later fruit loss.

steps: Remove and destroy severely affected vines when practical.
Prune during dry weather and avoid large pruning wounds.
Protect pruning cuts with wound sealants where recommended.
Improve vine vigor with balanced nutrition and proper irrigation.
prevention: Avoid mechanical injuries to trunks and cordons.
Practice good pruning hygiene by disinfecting tools between vines.
Remove and burn old, dead wood that can harbor pathogens.
Use certified clean planting material when establishing new vineyards.
Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).expertNote: Esca is a complex trunk disease; management focuses on prevention, vine vigor, and removal of heavily diseased plants.

steps: Remove and destroy trees confirmed with severe greening symptoms.
Control psyllid vectors using recommended insecticides and biological control.
Prune only lightly to reduce stress on infected trees.
Follow local regulatory guidelines for sampling and disposal.
prevention: Plant only certified disease-free nursery stock.
Implement area-wide psyllid management with neighboring growers.
Monitor trees frequently for early symptoms and psyllid presence.
Keep groves weed-free to reduce alternative hosts for the vector.
Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).expertNote: Citrus greening is highly destructive and currently has no cure; vector management and use of healthy planting material are essential.

steps: Remove and destroy infected foliage and tubers immediately.
Apply protectant fungicides before favorable weather and repeat as needed.
Avoid overhead irrigation and irrigate early in the day when necessary.
Harvest promptly once vines are killed to limit tuber infection.
prevention: Use certified seed potatoes with known disease resistance.
Avoid planting potatoes near cull piles or volunteer potatoes.
Ensure good field drainage to reduce prolonged leaf wetness.
Rotate away from potatoes and tomatoes for at least 2–3 years.
Potato___Late_blight.expertNote: Late blight can spread extremely rapidly in cool, wet conditions. Proactive fungicide programs and sanitation are critical.

steps: Apply fungicides at tasseling or early silking when disease pressure is high.
Remove and incorporate crop residues after harvest to speed decomposition.
Improve field drainage and avoid excessive plant density.
Scout fields regularly, especially lower canopy leaves, for early lesions.
prevention: Plant resistant hybrids adapted to the local region.
Rotate with non-host crops to reduce inoculum levels.
Avoid continuous corn in high-risk fields.
Manage nitrogen to support healthy growth without promoting dense canopies.
Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.expertNote: Northern leaf blight slows photosynthesis and can reduce yield significantly when upper leaves are heavily infected before grain fill.

steps: Immediately remove and destroy infected plants and nearby debris.
Apply labeled fungicides on a tight schedule when outbreaks are detected.
Stake or cage plants to keep foliage off the ground and improve airflow.
Avoid working in fields when foliage is wet to prevent spreading spores.
prevention: Use disease-free transplants and avoid saving seed from infected plants.
Water at the base of plants and avoid overhead irrigation.
Provide adequate spacing between plants for good air circulation.
Monitor weather forecasts and scout frequently during cool, wet periods.
Tomato___Late_blight.expertNote: Tomato late blight can destroy foliage and fruit rapidly; early detection and immediate action greatly improve control.

steps: Maintain consistent watering, keeping soil moist but not waterlogged.
Fertilize according to soil test recommendations.
Mulch around plants to conserve moisture and reduce soil splashing.
Prune lower leaves to improve airflow and reduce disease risk.
prevention: Rotate out of tomatoes and other solanaceous crops every few years.
Inspect plants weekly for early signs of pests or disease.
Sanitize tools between plants, especially after pruning.
Choose varieties adapted to your climate and disease pressure.
Tomato___healthy.expertNote: Healthy tomato plants are resilient but still require regular monitoring and good cultural practices to stay productive.

steps: Follow recommended seeding rates to avoid overly dense stands.
Apply balanced fertilization based on soil testing.
Manage weeds to reduce competition and pest habitat.
Irrigate only when necessary and avoid prolonged saturated soils.
prevention: Rotate crops to reduce disease and nematode buildup.
Use high-quality, treated seed with good germination.
Select resistant varieties for local disease challenges.
Scout fields at key growth stages for emerging issues.
Soybean___healthy.expertNote: Even healthy soybeans benefit from proactive scouting and crop rotation to prevent future disease problems.
